pattern: Scanning the binary representation and counting how many times two adjacent bits are both set reveals exactly how many consecutive-set-bit pairs exist, so the answer is simply whether that count equals one.
hints: Look at the binary representation of the number one bit at a time, comparing each bit to the one right before it.
Count how many adjacent positions both hold a set bit -- three consecutive set bits in a row count as two overlapping pairs, not one.
The answer is true only when that total count of adjacent sset-bit pairs is exactly one, and false for zero, two, or more.

edge_inputs.description: exactly one pair of consecutive set bits
no consecutive set bits at all
the smallest possible input has no bits set
three consecutive set bits form two overlapping pairs, not one
two separate non-adjacent pairs of consecutive set bits
a single set bit can never form a pair

stress.description: random values across the full bound

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def consecutiveSetBits(self, n: int) -> bool:
        pairs = 0
        prev = 0
        x = n
        first = True
        while x > 0:
            bit = x & 1
            if not first and bit == 1 and prev == 1:
                pairs += 1
            prev = bit
            first = False
            x >>= 1
        return pairs == 1
